planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions just at or slightly above the soil surface in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; do not plant too deeply or the rhizome will rot.

directions: Plant rhizomes just at the soil surface in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; dwarf crested iris is one of the most charming native shade wildflowers, carpeting the woodland floor with colonies of pale lavender-blue iris flowers marked with white and yellow crests in early spring before the tree canopy fully opens. It grows naturally on wooded slopes, creek banks, and woodland paths across the eastern US. The low-growing, grass-like foliage spreads quickly by shallow rhizome to form dense, weed-suppressing mats — an excellent living mulch under trees. Plant where you can enjoy the flowers up close. The plant goes semi-dormant in summer heat but greens up in fall. Divide crowded colonies in late summer or early fall. The white form 'Alba' is occasionally available.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water (1–4 inches) or saturated soil at pond or stream margins in partial shade once soil warms; requires consistent moisture and will not survive drought.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water or saturated, slightly acidic soil at pond or stream margins in partial shade; lizard's tail is a distinctive native aquatic perennial of southeastern swamps, stream banks, and freshwater wetlands, producing arching spikes of tiny white fragrant flowers that droop at the tip like a lizard's tail in midsummer, above large glossy heart-shaped leaves. It grows naturally in standing water and at stream margins from Quebec to Florida, and is an excellent choice for pond margins, boggy borders, and rain gardens where its attractive foliage and unusual flowers provide interest through the season. The plant spreads by rhizome to form naturalistic colonies. The fragrant flowers attract many small native bees and beetles. Excellent combined with pickerel rush and blue flag iris for a native wetland plant community.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in late summer to early fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; fall planting allows roots to establish before winter.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; copper iris is the only native American iris with brick-red to burnt-orange-copper flowers — a unique and striking color that no other wild iris can match. It grows naturally in swamps, bayou margins, and bottomland forests of the lower Mississippi Valley and Gulf Coast, where it tolerates periodic inundation. The plant is one of the parent species of the spectacular Louisiana iris hybrids and contributes their warm red-copper-orange color range to hybrid cultivars. Plant at pond margins, in consistently wet rain garden areas, or in soil that stays moist throughout the growing season. It spreads by rhizome into colonies and can be divided in late summer to expand plantings.

planting.note: Plant small container-grown trees (1–2 gallon) in winter or early spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; live oak establishes best when young. Large container or balled-and-burlapped trees are slow to re-establish. Plant acorns immediately upon collecting in fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; live oak is the defining tree of the Gulf Coast landscape, forming magnificent broad, sweeping canopies of twisted horizontal branches draped with Spanish moss, with a single mature tree providing habitat for hundreds of species of insects, birds, and wildlife. The leaves are semi-evergreen in zone 8 (dropping briefly in late winter as new leaves emerge) and evergreen in zone 9–10. It tolerates salt spray, sandy soils, and periodic drought once established and grows steadily — 1–2 feet per year when young. The small acorns are critically important fall and winter food for blue jays, wild turkeys, squirrels, and deer. Plant far from structures and utility lines — mature trees reach 40–60 feet tall with 60–100 foot spread. A heritage landscape tree meant for centuries.

planting.note: Plant small container trees in winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to wet soil; water oak is fast growing for an oak (2–3 feet per year when young) and establishes readily from planting.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically wet soil in full sun; water oak is one of the most common and fast-growing native oaks of the coastal South, easily identified by its distinctively variable leaf shape — three-lobed duck-foot leaves, paddle-shaped leaves, and narrow elliptic leaves can all appear on the same tree. It grows naturally in bottomland forests, wet stream banks, and moist upland soils from New Jersey to Texas and is among the fastest-growing oaks in the South (2–3 feet annually). The very small, nearly round acorns produced in enormous quantities beginning at 10–15 years are critically important for wood ducks, mallards, wild turkeys, squirrels, and deer. Water oak is smaller than live oak (40–70 feet) and better suited to residential landscapes where space is limited. It typically lives 60–80 years.
